coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of sulfide based on the self-assembly of fluorescent silver nanoclusters on the surface of silica nanospheres
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yehan
Zhang, Kui
Yu, Huan
Zhu, Houjuan
Sun, Mingtai
Hayat, Tasawar
Alsaedi, Ahmed
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen sulfide is a toxic and flammable gaseous pollutant often emitted to air as a by-product of water supply, chemical, petroleum and coal industries. It can be transferred into sulfur dioxide in the air under some meteorologic conditions. Herein, we report a novel ratiometric fluorescence method for hydrogen sulfide based on silver nanoclusters and quantum dots. The silver nanoclusters have been self-assemblied onto the surface of silica spheres, which are embedded with red fluorescent quantum dots, to form a dual-emissive nanohybrid. Such dual-emissive nanohybrid has been applied for hydrogen sulfide detection on the basis of the interfacial interaction between silver nanoclusters and sulfide ions. The blue-emission of Ag NCs is specifically prone to hydrogen sulfide due to surface binding and etching, but the red-emission of QDs within the silica nanospheres is inert against hydrogen sulfide. The different response of the two components to hydrogen sulfide results in fluorescence color variation from violet to red when the blue fluorescence is gradually quenched. This nanohybrid has been successfully demonstrated for the application in sensitive and selective detection of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solution and gaseous state.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen sulfide
Sensitive detection
Gaseous environmental pollutant
Silver nanocluster
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the red fluorescent CdTe QDs
Mercapto modified QDs embedded silica nanospheres (HS-CdTe@SiO2)
Synthesis of Ag nanoclusters (Ag NCs) and the self-assemblied nanohybrid probe
Characterization of the as-prepared Ag NCs and dual-emissive nanohybrid
Kinetic response of the nanohybrid for sulfide
The sensitivity of the nanohybrid for sulfide
The selectivity of the nanohybrid for sulfide detection
The visual detection of hydrogen sulfide gas

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21475134, 91439101, and 21507135) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2016ZZD06).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of the as-prepared Ag NCs (a), colloidal QDs@SiO2 nanospheres (c) and the nanohybrid probe (e). The fluorescence spectra and fluorescence images of Ag NCs (b), QDs@SiO2 nanospheres (d) and the nanohybrid probe (f).

(a) Effect of the exposure time for the fluorescence ratio I446/I626 of the nanohybrid in the presence of different concentration of sulfide ions. (b) The plot of fluorescence intensity ratio versus the concentration of added sulfide.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of the dual-emissive nanohybrid probe and (b) the single Ag NCs upon exposure to various concentrations of sulfide for 5min. The concentrations of sulfide from up to down were 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 and 3.0μM, respectively. The inset photos are their corresponding photographs taken under a 365 UV light.

(a) The fluorescence ratio I446/I626 of the nanohyrbid upon adding various anions. The concentrations are 3μM for sulfide and 50μM for other anions. (b) Corresponding photographs of the probe solution after adding the same level of these anions (3μM) independently.

The fluorescence images of the indicating bottles for visual detection of H2S gas. The H2S concentrations from left to right were 0, 0.1, 1, 5, 10 and 15ppm, respectively. The color change is distinct from left to right.

(a) The synthetic route of the mercapto functionalized QDs@SiO2 nanospheres colloid (QDs@SiO2 is a common symbol for a core-shell nano-structure, in which the core is quantum dots and the shell is silica, in this work, it can be replaced by CdTe@SiO2 for clarity); (b) the surface self-assembly of fluorescent silver nanoclusters onto the colloidal silica nanospheres and the formation of dual-emissive nanohybrid probe.

The structure of the nanohybrid with independent dual emissions and the principle for detecting sulfide ions. The bottom panel displays the fluorescence spectra and color of the nanohybrid before and after exposure to sulfide ions.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of sulfide based on the self-assembly of fluorescent silver nanoclusters on the surface of silica nanospheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering, North China Electric Power University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NAAM Research Group, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NAAM Research Group, King Abdulaziz University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics, Quaid-I-Azam University, Islamabad 44000, Pakistan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environment and Chemical Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen sulfide is a toxic and flammable gaseous pollutant often emitted to air as a by-product of water supply, chemical, petroleum and coal industries. It can be transferred into sulfur dioxide in the air under some meteorologic conditions. Herein, we report a novel ratiometric fluorescence method for hydrogen sulfide based on silver nanoclusters and quantum dots. The silver nanoclusters have been self-assemblied onto the surface of silica spheres, which are embedded with red fluorescent quantum dots, to form a dual-emissive nanohybrid. Such dual-emissive nanohybrid has been applied for hydrogen sulfide detection on the basis of the interfacial interaction between silver nanoclusters and sulfide ions. The blue-emission of Ag NCs is specifically prone to hydrogen sulfide due to surface binding and etching, but the red-emission of QDs within the silica nanospheres is inert against hydrogen sulfide. The different response of the two components to hydrogen sulfide results in fluorescence color variation from violet to red when the blue fluorescence is gradually quenched. This nanohybrid has been successfully demonstrated for the application in sensitive and selective detection of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solution and gaseous state.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfide compounds are environmental toxins and pollutants often emitted in the production of sulfur, sulfuric acid, dyes and cosmetics, decomposition of biomass, water supply, chemical, petroleum and coal industries, and etc. The excessive emission of sulfide contributes to serious environmental problems and is harmful to human's health. It has been assumed to be associated with many diseases, including Huntington's, Parkinson's, Alzheimer's diseases and even some cancers [1–3]. It becomes more poisonous when sulfide is protonated to release gaseous hydrogen sulfide (H2S) because it is inhalable and can quickly numb the olfactory sensation. Therefore, it is significant to develop novel methods for sensitive and selective detection of sulfide for monitoring its emission and environment pollution.

The currently used methods for sulfide determination include ion chromatography, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy, electrochemical analysis, and colorimetric assay [4–8]. Besides, fluorescence methods have been widely used due to their easy operation, high sensitivity, and simple instrumentation [9–13]. To date, the fluorescent probes for H2S by using its unique chemical properties of reducibility and nucleophilicity. As presented in Table S1, these probes have been designed mainly rely on three reaction mechanisms: azide reduction, nucleophilic reaction and the formation of sulfide precipitation [14–25]. Most of the probes are organic molecular fluorescence probes like coumarin, BODIPY, rhodamine, terpyridine, fluorescein, porphyrin and copper complex and show good sensitivity and selectivity. These organic fluorescence probes are generally synthesized through sophisticated procedures and readily prone to photobleaching. Furthermore, many of them only have single emission, which are dependent on conditions such as probe concentration, temperature, pH value and etc.

Recently, increasing efforts have been devoted to the integration of different colloidal nanomaterial at their interface to achieve new strategies for environmental and biological analysis [26–33]. Silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) consist of hundreds of silver atoms and exhibit some attractive properties like surface reactivity, tunable fluorescence intensity, good photostability, and excellent water-solubility [34]. Such properties have been adopted in application for sensing metal ions [35,36], small biomolecules [37,38], and ribonucleic acids [39]. Similarly, semiconductor QDs also have some unique properties including size-tunable emission, broad excitation bands, symmetric fluorescence spectra [40,41]. These superior properties of QDs offer significant advantages as optical labels for chemo/bio-sensing and immunosensing [42,43]. As for optical labels, however, the QDs should be protected into inert outer layer such as silica shell for their fluorescence stability and their surface could be further manipulated for specific functional groups [44,45].

In this work, glutathione-functionalized silver nanoclusters with bright blue fluorescence were first synthesized and followed by self-assembling onto the thiol-functional surface of colloidal silica nanospheres (CdTe@SiO2), which were embedded with red fluorescent CdTe QDs. These red QDs were encapsulated in the inert silica shell to improve their stability. The colloidal silica shell was functionalized with mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTS) to induce the self-assembly of silver nanoclusters onto the interface, which finally produced a nanohybrid with independent dual-emission from the two components. The component of Ag NCs is very reactive to hydrogen sulfide and its fluorescence can be greatly quenched. However, the red-colored fluorescence of CdTe@SiO2 cannot be influenced by sulfide and acts as reference signal. Such dual-emissive nanohybrid could be applied for sulfide ion detection based on the ratiometric fluorescence signal.

Therefore, we integrated the inorganic blue fluorescent Ag NCs with red fluorescent QDs into a dual-emission nanohybrid through interfacial self-assembly for selective and sensitive detection of sulfide. Such independently dual-emissive fluorescence nanohybrid probe can avoid interference on the basis of the built-in correction. The nanohybrid shows a limit of detection of 32nM for sulfide in aqueous solution. We have also demonstrated the fabrication of indicating bottles for visually monitoring of gaseous hydrogen sulfide by the appearance of a different fluorescence color, showing the potential in practical application for on-site detection of hydrogen sulfide.

Reduced glutathione (GSH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), sodium sulfide (Na2S), sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bromide (NaBr), potassium iodide (KI), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), sodium bisulfite (NaHSO3), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate solution (NaHCO3), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), tert-Butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) and L-cysteine (L-Cys) were received from Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. The ultrapure water was prepared from a Millipore water purification system with a resistivity 18.2MΩcm.

Fluorescence spectra, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer, Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer, and Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer, respectively. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope. Fluorescence photos were taken using a Canon 350D digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the red fluorescent CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilic CdTe QDs using MPA as the stabilizer were prepared according to our previous method [46,47]. In a typical way, 31.9mg of Te powder and 0.05g of NaBH4 were added into 3mL of H2O under nitrogen atmosphere in at ice bath. After stirring for 6h, the black disappeared and the precursor of NaHTe formed. On the other hand, 114.2mg of CdCl2·2.5H2O and 105μL of MPA were dissolved in120 mL of H2O and adjusting the pH value of the mixture to 10 with 1.0M NaOH to prepared the cadmium precursor. Then, the mixture was deoxygenated by bubbling nitrogen for 30min. Finally, the freshly NaHTe was transferred to the cadmium precursor solution (final molar ratio, Cd2+/Te2−/MPA = 1:0.5:2.5) under nitrogen atmosphere, and the color of the mixture solution immediately changed from colorless to orange. The fluorescence color or emission wavelength of CdTe quantum dots can be readily tuned from green to red by controlling their sizes and surface states during the synthetic process, because the band gap of quantum dots is size dependent. The red CdTe quantum dots in the manuscript were obtained by refluxing for 48h at 110°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mercapto modified QDs embedded silica nanospheres (HS-CdTe@SiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: The red fluorescent QDs stabilized by wrapping QDs in silica nanospheres through a classical Stöber method [48]. Briefly, 5mL of red QDs, 15mL of H2O, 40mL of ethanol and 20μL of MPTS were added into a flask under stirring for 6h at room temperature. Subsequently, 500μL of NH3·H2O and 500μL of TEOS were introduced into the mixture, the reaction system was then sealed and stirred in the dark for 12h to obtain the colloidal silica nanospheres with a core-shell structure of CdTe@SiO2. To further functionalize the silica interface with mercapto groups, 15μL of MPTS was added to the above solution. After reaction for more 6h, the resultant precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol and ultrapure water. It was then re-dispersed in water to get the mercapto modified silica nanospheres colloid (HS-CdTe@SiO2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag nanoclusters (Ag NCs) and the self-assemblied nanohybrid probe
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the synthetic route of the self-assemblied nanohybrid. First, the solution of AgNO3 (2.5mL, 25mM) was slowly added to GSH solution (10mL, 25mM) in a flask under vigorous stirring. The solution quickly became turbid due to the formation of white silver thiolate precipitate. The pH value of the solution was then adjusted to 5 with 1.0M NaOH and kept stirring for 4h. The as-prepared Ag NCs was obtained by dialyzing the solution against pure water using a membrane (MWCO = 1.0kDa) for 6h and then kept at 4°C for further use.

The self-assemblied nanohybrid probe were received by mixing the as-prepared Ag NCs with the silica nanospheres embedded QDs (HS-CdTe@SiO2) in aqueous solution. The mixture was stirred for 2h for the Ag NCs to self-assembly onto the surface of the colloidal silica nanospheres through the conjugation between –SH and the surface Ag atoms.

A variety of concentrations of sulfide were first added into the nanohybrid solution. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were then recorded from 395 to 700nm under excitation at 350nm. The relative fluorescence intensity I446/I626 of the nanohybrid as a function of the sulfide concentration was used for the calibration. The solution (10mM) of other anionic species (F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, HPO4 2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, H2PO4 -, HCO3 -, HSO3 -) were prepared directly by dissolving their corresponding sodium or potassium salts in ultrapure water. These anions were used for selectivity study. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 5min after addition of each of the anionic ion solution into the nanohybrid solution in a quartz cuvette.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared Ag NCs and dual-emissive nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a facile one-pot synthesis of fluorescent Ag NCs was achieved in the presence of GSH, which served as reducing and stabilizing agent. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to evaluate the shape and size of the as-prepared Ag NCs. The Ag NCs have relatively uniform size of 2nm and no aggregation formed, as depicted in Fig. 1a. A bright blue emission centered at 446nm under excitation at 350nm can be observed (Fig. 1b). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been carefully studied to investigate the oxidation states of Ag atoms in the Ag NCs. As shown in Fig. S1(a), two peaks located at the binding energies of 367.8 and 373.8eV, which are consistent with 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 photoelectrons of Ag. The slightly broad peak at 367.8eV was then deconvoluted into one prominent peak at 367.7eV (Fig. S1(b)), accompanied with a small peak at 367.9eV, which are attributed to the Ag(0) and Ag(I) atoms [49,50], respectively. The predominant peak of Ag(0) confirmed the successful formation of Ag NCs. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra also evidenced the S 2p peak at 163.5 and 164.6eV (Fig. S1(c)) from the as-synthesized AgNCs, which could be attributed to the formation of disulfide linkage and stability to the cluster. In addition, the FT-IR spectra of GSH and Ag NCs (Fig. S2) showed that the featured S–H stretching band at 2524.7cm-1 of GSH disappeared, indicating the formation of covalent bonds between the thiol group and the Ag NCs [51].

The CdTe QDs were encased in an inert silica shell to form the colloidal CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres, which were also characterized by TEM. The average diameter of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres was measured to be 55nm (Fig. 1c). The suspension of CdTe@SiO2 nanospheres presents a strong red fluorescence as seen in Fig. 1d. Its fluorescence spectra were subsequently recorded each 5min for 1h (Fig. S3). Clearly, the fluorescence intensity keeps constant during the measurement, implying its good stability against photobleaching.

The nanohybrid spheres have the similar size to the colloidal silica nanospheres of about 55nm (Fig. 1e). It shows two emissions centered at 446 and 626nm, respectively. It appears a composite violet blue color under the excitation of UV lamp (Fig. 1f). The structure and principle of the nanohybrid for visual detection of sulfide are shown in Scheme 2. We can see that the blue fluorescence of Ag NCs is selectively quenched by sulfide and acts as the specific receptor signal. Meanwhile, the red fluorescence of CdTe QDs embedded in silica sphere is inert to sulfide, serves as reference signal and provides built-in correction. Consequently, the different responses of the two components lead to the variation of the fluorescence intensity ratio of I446/I626 depending on the amount of sulfide ions hence causing gradual color change from violet blue to red. The property can be used for detecting sulfide. The stability of the nanohybrid solution was systematically investigated (Fig. S4), the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I446/I626) of the solution has no significant change, indicating such nanohybrid has a good stability against photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic response of the nanohybrid for sulfide
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic behavior between the nanohybrid and sulfide was first examined ( Fig. 2a) by recording the relative fluorescence intensity I446/I626 as a function of exposure time. It is clear that the ratio intensity I446/I626 of nanohybrid decreased dramatically within 3min upon exposure to sulfide and reached constant after 5min. So it was suggested that the reaction between the nanohybrid and sulfide needs 5min to reach the equilibrium, and the reaction time was used in subsequent experiments. The dose-response of the nanohybrid to different concentrations of sulfide was presented in Fig. 2b. A correlation linear plot of I446/I626 versus sulfide concentrations in the range from 0 to 3μM was established (I446/I626 = 5.857 − 1.843 [S2-], R2 = 0.996). The lowest concentration of the method can reach 32nM based on the definition of LOD= 3 × S.D./k, where k is the slope of the curve equation, and S.D. represents the standard deviation for the dual-emission solution's fluorescence intensity ratio in the absence of sulfide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity of the nanohybrid for sulfide
ce:section.ce:para: The nanohybrid shows two emissions centered at 446 and 626nm, which are ascribed to the emission from blue fluorescence of Ag NCs and red fluorescence of CdTe QDs encapsulated in silica spheres, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, the blue fluorescence from the component of Ag NCs was greatly quenched by sulfide, whereas the red emission from QDs embedded silica stays almost unchanged. The variation of the intensity ratio I446/I626 by self-calibration results in successive color change and can be clearly distinguished from the original background with the naked eyes. For comparison, the blue fluorescence of single Ag NCs decreased only in intensity without any color variation upon sulfide, which is hard to be recognized by our naked eyes (Fig. 3b). Therefore, the nanohybrid with dual-emission has more advantages by utilizing an internal standard, which could produce a broader color grading and more reliable for visual detection of target analyte.

The quenching mechanism for silver nanocluster by sulfide is ascribed to the surface defects due to the formation of Ag2S on surface [51,52]. The surface Ag(I) is reactive to sulfide and form a surface defect, leading to the fluorescence quenching. To further investigate the reaction, the absorption spectra of Ag NCs were examined in the absence of and presence of sulfide (Fig. S5). Clearly, the specific absorption band at 350nm of Ag NCs decreased and a broad absorbance band for the Ag2S particles appeared, accompanying by the color of the solution turned into yellow with the increase amount of sulfide, confirming the production of Ag2S layer on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the nanohybrid for sulfide detection
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the nanohybrid for sulfide, other relevant anions (including F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, HPO4 2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, H2PO4 -, HCO3 -, HSO3 -, each at a concentration of 50μM) were each added to the probe solution ( Fig. 4a) and fluorescence spectra were recorded, respectively. The results show that no conspicuous change in the fluorescence ratio of I446/I626 was observed. However, when sulfide solution (3μM) was subsequently added into each mixture, the fluorescence gradually changed in the similar way to those of sulfide (Fig. S6). The results indicated that the nanohybrid is selectively reactive to sulfide and other co-existence of these species has negligible interfering effect on sulfide sensing. In addition, some reactive oxygen species and thiol molecules (NO3 -, NO2 -, H2O2, HClO, TBHP, GSH, and Cys, each at a concentration of 10μM) were also measured (Fig. S7). The results show that no significant change in the ratio I446/I626 of the nanohybrid probe can be observed, revealing that the nanohybrid has a good selectivity for sulfide.

Furthermore, in order to study the selectivity of the nanohybrid for gaseous H2S detection, we first examined its fluorescence response to some possible background gases including SO2, CO, NO2, CO2 and NH3. The results were shown in Fig. S8. Clearly, these competitive gaseous species did not generate remarkable change of the fluorescence intensity. However, when the nanohybrid was exposed to the same amount of gaseous H2S, the fluorescence intensity at 446nm of the probe greatly declined, along with its fluorescence image changing to red, indicating its good selectivity for H2S gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visual detection of hydrogen sulfide gas
ce:section.ce:para: We further demonstrated the successful fabrication of indicating bottle for monitoring H2S gas. In the experiment, the gaseous H2S was first generated from the reaction of phosphoric acid and sodium sulfide, and was diluted for experimental usage in a 100mL round-bottomed flask. The indicating bottles were prepared by transferring 1.5mL of the nanohybrid suspension into each vial 2.5mL in volume, with a rubber cap. For visual detection of H2S gas, 1.0mL of the gas samples containing different levels of H2S were bubbled into each indicating bottle with a syringe through the rubber cap. After 5min reaction, and fluorescence photos of each indicating bottles were taken under the illumination of a 365nm UV light ( Fig. 5). It can be seen that the fluorescence color evolution from violet blue to red which was related to the amount of H2S added. A relative low limit of detection of 0.1ppm was estimated, and the result indicates the method's potential application for visual detection of gaseous H2S.

We have fabricated a new nanohybrid probe by self-assembling fluorescent Ag NCs on the colloidal silica interface of QDs@SiO2 nanospheres. The nanohybrid shows relative stable dual-emissions at 446 and 626nm, respectively. A distinct color change from violet blue to red can be observed when the nanohybrid is exposed to sulfide. We have applied the nanohybrid probe and demonstrated for the sensitive and selective detection of hydrogen sulfide. In addition, the probe exhibits a good selectivity with a limit of detection of 32nM in aqueous solution, and the nanohybrid can be employed to make indicating bottles for rapid on-site determination of gaseous hydrogen sulfide.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21475134, 91439101, and 21507135) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2016ZZD06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.06.027.

Removal of dissolved sulfides in aqueous solution by activated sludge: mechanism and characteristics
Molecularly engineered quantum dots for visualization of hydrogen sulfide
Selectivity of layered double hydroxides and their derivative mixed metal oxides as sorbents of hydrogen sulfide

Visualizing gaseous nitrogen dioxide by ratiometric fluorescence of carbon nanodots −quantum dots hybrid

The mechanism for degrading orange II based on adsorption and reduction by ion-based nanoparticles synthesized by grape leaf extract
